title: Closure Capture
description:
Create a variable `name` set to `"Rust"`. Then create a closure `greet` that captures `name` and returns a greeting string. Call the closure and print the result.

Expected output:
```
Hello, Rust!
```
hint: A closure can capture variables from its environment. Use || format!("Hello, {}!", name) to create the closure.

steps.title: Define the variable
steps.explanation: We create a string slice `name` that the closure will capture from its environment.

steps.title: Create the closure
steps.explanation: The closure `|| format!("Hello, {}!", name)` captures `name` by reference and returns a formatted String.

steps.title: Call and print
steps.explanation: Calling greet() executes the closure, which produces "Hello, Rust!".

title: Apply Twice
description:
Write a function `apply_twice(f: impl Fn(i32) -> i32, x: i32) -> i32` that applies function `f` to `x` two times: `f(f(x))`.

Example: applying a function that adds 3 to the value 10 twice gives 16.

Expected output:
```
16
```
hint: Call f(x) to get the first result, then pass that result to f again.

steps.title: Accept a function parameter
steps.explanation: The `impl Fn(i32) -> i32` trait bound means f can be any closure or function that takes an i32 and returns an i32.
steps.code: fn apply_twice(f: impl Fn(i32) -> i32, x: i32) -> i32 {

steps.title: Apply twice
steps.explanation: First call f(x) = 10 + 3 = 13, then f(13) = 13 + 3 = 16. The nested call f(f(x)) chains both.

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: add3(10) = 13, add3(13) = 16. Output is 16.

title: Custom Filter
description:
Write a function `keep_if(nums: &[i32], pred: impl Fn(i32) -> bool) -> Vec<i32>` that returns only elements where `pred` returns true.

Filter the list `[1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]` to keep only even numbers.

Expected output:
```
[2, 4, 6]
```
hint: Iterate over nums, dereference each element, and check if pred(*n) is true. Collect matching elements into a Vec.

steps.title: Iterate and copy
steps.explanation: Use iter().copied() to get owned i32 values from the slice reference.

steps.title: Filter with predicate
steps.explanation: The filter adapter keeps only elements where pred(x) returns true. We pass the closure from the caller.

steps.title: Collect results
steps.explanation: collect() gathers the filtered values into a Vec<i32>. Output is [2, 4, 6].

title: Function Pointer
description:
Write two functions: `double(x: i32) -> i32` that returns `x * 2`, and `triple(x: i32) -> i32` that returns `x * 3`. Store them in a vector of function pointers and apply each to the value 5.

Expected output:
```
10
15
```
hint: Function pointers in Rust have the type `fn(args) -> return`. Named functions can be stored directly as function pointers.

steps.title: Define functions
steps.explanation: double and triple are regular functions that can be used as function pointers since they capture no environment.

steps.title: Store as pointers
steps.explanation: Vec<fn(i32) -> i32> holds function pointers. We push double and triple into the vector.
steps.code: let ops: Vec<fn(i32) -> i32> = vec![double, triple];

steps.title: Iterate and call
steps.explanation: For each function pointer op, we call op(5). double(5)=10, triple(5)=15.

title: Map Reduce
description:
Write a function `map_reduce(nums: &[i32], map_fn: impl Fn(i32) -> i32, reduce_fn: impl Fn(i32, i32) -> i32, init: i32) -> i32` that first maps each element then reduces (folds) them.

Square each element of `[1, 2, 3, 4]` then sum them starting from 0.

Expected output:
```
30
```
hint: Chain iter().copied().map(map_fn).fold(init, reduce_fn) to first transform then accumulate.

steps.title: Map each element
steps.explanation: iter().copied().map(map_fn) applies the squaring closure to each element: 1, 4, 9, 16.

steps.title: Fold to reduce
steps.explanation: fold(init, reduce_fn) accumulates: 0+1=1, 1+4=5, 5+9=14, 14+16=30.

title: Word Counter
description:
Write a function `count_words(words: &[&str]) -> std::collections::HashMap<String, usize>` that counts how many times each word appears.

Count words in `["apple", "banana", "apple", "cherry", "banana", "apple"]` and print the count for "apple".

Expected output:
```
apple: 3
```
hint: Use the entry API: *counts.entry(word.to_string()).or_insert(0) += 1;

steps.title: Create a HashMap
steps.explanation: We need a mutable HashMap<String, usize> to track word counts.

steps.title: Use the entry API
steps.explanation: entry() returns an Entry enum. or_insert(0) inserts 0 if the key is missing. We then increment the count.

steps.title: Look up result
steps.explanation: counts["apple"] returns 3 since apple appears three times in the input.

title: Sorted Keys
description:
Insert the key-value pairs ("cherry", 3), ("apple", 1), ("banana", 2) into a BTreeMap and print all entries. BTreeMap keeps keys sorted.

Expected output:
```
apple: 1
banana: 2
cherry: 3
```
hint: Use map.insert(key, value) to add entries. Iterate with a for loop over &map to print them in sorted order.

steps.title: Create BTreeMap
steps.explanation: BTreeMap stores entries sorted by key, unlike HashMap which has no guaranteed order.

steps.title: Insert entries
steps.explanation: Even though we insert cherry first, the BTreeMap will sort them alphabetically.

steps.title: Iterate in order
steps.explanation: Iterating over &map yields entries in key-sorted order: apple, banana, cherry.

title: Unique Items
description:
Write a function `unique(items: &[i32]) -> Vec<i32>` that returns the unique elements in sorted order using a HashSet.

Example: `unique(&[3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2, 6, 5, 3])` returns `[1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 9]`.

Expected output:
```
[1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 9]
```
hint: Collect into a HashSet to remove duplicates, then collect into a Vec and sort it.

steps.title: Collect into HashSet
steps.explanation: A HashSet automatically removes duplicates. We collect the slice elements into it.

steps.title: Convert and sort
steps.explanation: Convert the set back to a Vec, then sort it to get a deterministic order.
steps.code:
let mut result: Vec<i32> = set.into_iter().collect();
result.sort();

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: Duplicates (1, 5, 3) are removed. Sorted result is [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 9].

title: Queue Ops
description:
Use a VecDeque as a queue: push 1, 2, 3 to the back, then pop from the front one at a time and print each value.

Expected output:
```
1
2
3
```
hint: Use push_back() to enqueue and pop_front() to dequeue. pop_front() returns an Option, so use unwrap() or a while let loop.

steps.title: Create and fill queue
steps.explanation: VecDeque is a double-ended queue. push_back adds to the rear, giving us FIFO order.

steps.title: Pop from front
steps.explanation: pop_front() removes and returns the first element as Some(val), or None when empty.

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: Elements come out in insertion order: 1, 2, 3 — classic queue (FIFO) behavior.

title: Heap Sort
description:
Use a BinaryHeap to sort numbers. Push `[4, 1, 7, 3, 9, 2]` into a max-heap, then pop all elements to get them in descending order.

Expected output:
```
9 7 4 3 2 1
```
hint: BinaryHeap is a max-heap. Push all items, then pop() repeatedly. The largest element comes out first.

steps.title: Push elements
steps.explanation: Push all numbers into the BinaryHeap. It internally maintains the max-heap property.

steps.title: Pop in order
steps.explanation: pop() always removes the largest element. Repeated pops give descending order: 9, 7, 4, 3, 2, 1.

steps.title: Format output
steps.explanation: Join the results with spaces to produce "9 7 4 3 2 1".

title: Trait Shape
description:
Define a trait `Area` with a method `area(&self) -> f64`. Implement it for a `Circle` struct with field `radius: f64`. Create a circle with radius 5.0 and print its area.

Expected output:
```
78.54
```
hint: Use impl Area for Circle { fn area(&self) -> f64 { ... } }. The area formula is PI * r * r.

steps.title: Define the trait
steps.explanation: The Area trait declares an area() method that each shape must implement.

steps.title: Implement for Circle
steps.explanation: We use the formula PI * r^2. std::f64::consts::PI provides a precise value of pi.

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: PI * 5.0 * 5.0 = 78.5398... formatted to 2 decimal places gives 78.54.

title: Default Method
description:
Define a trait `Greetable` with a required method `name(&self) -> &str` and a default method `greet(&self) -> String` that returns `"Hello, <name>!"`.

Implement it for a `User` struct. Print the greeting.

Expected output:
```
Hello, Alice!
```
hint: Default methods have a body in the trait definition. Use format!("Hello, {}!", self.name()) in the default greet method.

steps.title: Default method
steps.explanation: The greet() method has a default implementation in the trait, so implementors only need to provide name().

steps.title: Implement required method
steps.explanation: User only needs to implement name(), returning a reference to the username field.

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: u.greet() calls the default method, which calls u.name() returning "Alice", producing "Hello, Alice!".

title: Multi Trait
description:
Define two traits: `HasName` with method `name(&self) -> &str` and `HasAge` with method `age(&self) -> u32`. Implement both for a `Person` struct. Write a function that requires both traits and prints a summary.

Expected output:
```
Bob is 30 years old
```
hint: Use `impl HasName + HasAge` or `T: HasName + HasAge` to require multiple trait bounds on a parameter.

steps.title: Implement traits
steps.explanation: Person implements both HasName (returning &self.name) and HasAge (returning self.age).
steps.code: impl HasName for Person { fn name(&self) -> &str { &self.name } }

steps.title: Combine trait bounds
steps.explanation: The + syntax combines multiple trait bounds. The function accepts any type that implements both traits.

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: describe(&p) calls both name() and age() on the Person, printing "Bob is 30 years old".

title: Trait Bounds
description:
Write a generic function `largest<T: PartialOrd>(list: &[T]) -> &T` that returns a reference to the largest element in a slice.

Find the largest in `[34, 50, 25, 100, 65]`.

Expected output:
```
100
```
hint: Start with the first element as the current largest. Loop through the rest, updating when you find a bigger element.

steps.title: Generic with bounds
steps.explanation: T: PartialOrd means T must support comparison with >. This works for i32, f64, and many other types.

steps.title: Find maximum
steps.explanation: Start with the first element, then iterate through the rest. Update max whenever we find a larger element.

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: Comparing 34, 50, 25, 100, 65: the largest is 100.

title: Display Impl
description:
Define a `Color` struct with fields `r: u8, g: u8, b: u8`. Implement the `std::fmt::Display` trait to format it as `rgb(r, g, b)`. Print a color.

Expected output:
```
rgb(255, 128, 0)
```
hint: Implement fmt::Display by writing an impl block with fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter) -> fmt::Result, using write!(f, ...) inside.

steps.title: Implement Display
steps.explanation: The Display trait requires a fmt method. We use write!() to format the output string.

steps.title: Use with println!
steps.explanation: Now println!("{}", c) calls our Display implementation, formatting the Color nicely.

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: Color { r: 255, g: 128, b: 0 } displays as "rgb(255, 128, 0)".

title: Shared Refs
description:
Create a vector of numbers `[10, 20, 30]`. Create two shared (immutable) references to it and use both to print the sum and the length.

Expected output:
```
Sum: 60
Length: 3
```
hint: Multiple shared references (&vec) can exist at the same time. Use iter().sum::<i32>() for the sum and .len() for the length.

steps.title: Create shared references
steps.explanation: Rust allows multiple immutable (&) references to exist simultaneously — this is safe because no one can modify the data.

steps.title: Use both references
steps.explanation: r1 is used to compute the sum via iter().sum(), and r2 is used to get the length.
steps.code:
let total: i32 = r1.iter().sum();
println!("Length: {}", r2.len());

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: 10 + 20 + 30 = 60 and the vector has 3 elements.

title: Mutable Ref
description:
Create a mutable vector `[1, 2, 3]`. Write a function `double_all(nums: &mut Vec<i32>)` that doubles each element in place. Print the vector after calling it.

Expected output:
```
[2, 4, 6]
```
hint: Iterate over the vector with iter_mut() and multiply each element by 2 using *n *= 2.

steps.title: Mutable reference parameter
steps.explanation: The &mut Vec<i32> parameter lets us modify the vector in place. Only one mutable reference can exist at a time.

steps.title: Modify in place
steps.explanation: iter_mut() gives mutable references to each element. *n *= 2 dereferences and doubles each value.

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: After doubling: 1*2=2, 2*2=4, 3*2=6. Output is [2, 4, 6].

title: Lifetime Intro
description:
Write a function `longer<'a>(s1: &'a str, s2: &'a str) -> &'a str` that returns the longer of two string slices. If equal, return the first.

Compare "hello" and "world!".

Expected output:
```
world!
```
hint: Compare s1.len() and s2.len(). Return s2 only if it is strictly longer, otherwise return s1.

steps.title: Lifetime annotation
steps.explanation: The 'a lifetime tells the compiler that the returned reference lives as long as both inputs. This is needed because the compiler can't know which branch will be taken.
steps.code: fn longer<'a>(s1: &'a str, s2: &'a str) -> &'a str {

steps.title: Compare lengths
steps.explanation: We compare string lengths. "hello" has 5 chars, "world!" has 6, so s2 is longer.
steps.code: if s2.len() > s1.len() { s2 } else { s1 }

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: "world!" (6 chars) is longer than "hello" (5 chars), so we get "world!".

title: String Slices
description:
Write a function `first_word(s: &str) -> &str` that returns the first word of a string (up to the first space, or the whole string if no space).

Expected output:
```
Hello
```
hint: Use find(' ') to locate the first space, then slice with &s[..index]. If no space, return the entire string.

steps.title: Find the space
steps.explanation: s.find(' ') returns Some(index) if a space is found, or None if there is no space.

steps.title: Slice the string
steps.explanation: &s[..i] takes a slice from the start up to (but not including) the space. This gives us the first word.

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: "Hello World Rust" has a space at index 5. &s[..5] is "Hello".

title: Split Borrow
description:
Create a mutable vector `[1, 2, 3, 4, 5]`. Use `split_at_mut` to get two mutable slices — one for the first 2 elements and one for the rest. Double the first slice and triple the second. Print the full vector.

Expected output:
```
[2, 4, 9, 12, 15]
```
hint: split_at_mut(2) returns two mutable slices. You can iterate each with iter_mut() and modify independently.

steps.title: Split the vector
steps.explanation: split_at_mut(2) gives two non-overlapping mutable slices. This is safe because the slices don't overlap.

steps.title: Modify each half
steps.explanation: Double the left slice [1, 2] -> [2, 4] and triple the right slice [3, 4, 5] -> [9, 12, 15].
steps.code:
for n in left.iter_mut() { *n *= 2; }
for n in right.iter_mut() { *n *= 3; }

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: The full vector is now [2, 4, 9, 12, 15] — both halves were modified independently.

title: Question Mark
description:
Write a function `parse_and_double(s: &str) -> Result<i32, std::num::ParseIntError>` that parses a string to i32 and doubles it using the `?` operator.

Parse "21" and print the doubled result.

Expected output:
```
42
```
hint: Use s.parse::<i32>()? to parse with automatic error propagation. Then multiply by 2 and wrap in Ok().

steps.title: Parse with ?
steps.explanation: The ? operator extracts the Ok value or returns the Err early. s.parse::<i32>()? either gives us the number or propagates the error.

steps.title: Double and return
steps.explanation: If parsing succeeded, we multiply by 2 and wrap in Ok(). "21" parses to 21, doubled is 42.

steps.title: Handle in main
steps.explanation: We use match to handle both Ok and Err cases. With "21" as input, we get Ok(42).

title: Custom Error
description:
Define a custom error enum `MathError` with variants `DivisionByZero` and `Negative`. Write a function `safe_sqrt(x: f64) -> Result<f64, MathError>` that returns an error for negative input.

Compute the square root of 16.0.

Expected output:
```
4
```
hint: Check if x < 0.0 and return Err(MathError::Negative). Otherwise return Ok(x.sqrt()).

steps.title: Define error type
steps.explanation: The MathError enum has variants for different error cases. This is idiomatic Rust error handling.

steps.title: Validate and compute
steps.explanation: Check for negative input first. If valid, compute sqrt. 16.0.sqrt() = 4.0.

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: 16.0 is not negative, so we get Ok(4.0). println! formats 4.0 as "4".

title: Error Display
description:
Define an error enum `AppError` with a variant `NotFound(String)`. Implement `std::fmt::Display` for it. Create an error and print it.

Expected output:
```
Not found: config.toml
```
hint: Implement fmt::Display with a match on self. For NotFound(name), write "Not found: {name}".

steps.title: Define error enum
steps.explanation: AppError::NotFound holds a String with the name of whatever was not found.

steps.title: Implement Display
steps.explanation: Match on the variant and use write! to format the error message.

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: The error displays as "Not found: config.toml" — a human-readable error message.

title: From Trait
description:
Define an error enum `ConvertError` with a variant `ParseFailed(String)`. Implement `From<std::num::ParseIntError>` for it so parse errors convert automatically. Parse "abc" and handle the error.

Expected output:
```
Conversion error: ParseFailed("invalid digit found in string")
```
hint: Implement From<ParseIntError> for ConvertError by converting the error's message with e.to_string() into ParseFailed.

steps.title: Implement From
steps.explanation: The From trait lets the ? operator automatically convert ParseIntError into our ConvertError.

steps.title: Use ? for conversion
steps.explanation: When parse() fails, ? calls From::from() on the error, converting it to ConvertError::ParseFailed.

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: "abc" can't parse as i32, so we get ConvertError::ParseFailed with the parse error message.

title: Unwrap Or
description:
Write a function `safe_divide(a: f64, b: f64) -> Option<f64>` that returns None for division by zero. Use `unwrap_or` to provide a default value of 0.0.

Divide 10.0 by 0.0 and 10.0 by 4.0, printing both results.

Expected output:
```
0
2.5
```
hint: Check if b is 0.0 (use b == 0.0). Return None if so, otherwise return Some(a / b).

steps.title: Check for zero
steps.explanation: Return None when dividing by zero instead of panicking or producing infinity.

steps.title: Return result
steps.explanation: For valid inputs, return Some(a / b). 10.0 / 4.0 = 2.5.

steps.title: Use unwrap_or
steps.explanation: unwrap_or(0.0) extracts the value from Some, or returns 0.0 for None. First call gives 0, second gives 2.5.

title: Type Alias
description:
Create a type alias `type Matrix = Vec<Vec<i32>>`. Write a function `sum_matrix(m: &Matrix) -> i32` that sums all elements. Sum a 2x2 matrix.

Expected output:
```
10
```
hint: Use nested iteration: m.iter() for rows, then row.iter() for elements. Sum all elements with flat_map or a fold.

steps.title: Define type alias
steps.explanation: type Matrix = Vec<Vec<i32>> creates a shorter name for a nested vector. It's just an alias, not a new type.

steps.title: Flatten and sum
steps.explanation: flat_map flattens the rows into a single iterator of &i32 references, then sum() adds them all.

title: Newtype Wrap
description:
Create a newtype `Meters(f64)` and implement an `add` method that adds two Meters values. Add 3.5 meters and 2.5 meters.

Expected output:
```
6 meters
```
hint: Access the inner f64 with self.0 and other.0. Return a new Meters wrapping the sum.

steps.title: Newtype pattern
steps.explanation: Meters(f64) is a tuple struct wrapping a single f64. This gives type safety — you can't accidentally mix Meters with other f64 values.

steps.title: Add method
steps.explanation: Access the inner value with .0 and return a new Meters wrapping the sum.

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: 3.5 + 2.5 = 6.0. println! formats 6.0 as "6" so output is "6 meters".

title: Generic Pair
description:
Define a generic struct `Pair<T>` with fields `first: T` and `second: T`. Add a method `swap(&self) -> Pair<T>` where T: Clone that returns a new pair with swapped fields.

Expected output:
```
(world, hello)
```
hint: Return a new Pair with first set to self.second.clone() and second set to self.first.clone().

steps.title: Generic struct
steps.explanation: Pair<T> works with any type T. Both fields must be the same type.
steps.code: struct Pair<T> { first: T, second: T }

steps.title: Clone-bounded swap
steps.explanation: T: Clone is needed because we're creating new copies of the values in swapped positions.

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: Swapping ("hello", "world") gives ("world", "hello").

title: Where Clause
description:
Write a function using a where clause: `fn print_info<T>(item: &T) where T: std::fmt::Debug + std::fmt::Display` that prints both the Debug and Display forms.

Print info for the number 42.

Expected output:
```
Display: 42
Debug: 42
```
hint: Use println! with {} for Display and {:?} for Debug formatting.

steps.title: Where clause syntax
steps.explanation: The where clause puts trait bounds after the function signature. This is cleaner for complex bounds.

steps.title: Use both traits
steps.explanation: {} invokes Display and {:?} invokes Debug. For i32, both produce the same output.

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: For 42, both Display and Debug format as "42".

title: Phantom Type
description:
Use PhantomData to create type-safe unit markers. Define empty structs `Km` and `Miles`, and a `Distance<U>` struct that holds an f64 value. Create a distance in Km and one in Miles and print both.

Expected output:
```
100 km
62.14 miles
```
hint: The phantom type parameter U doesn't affect runtime — it's only for compile-time type checking. Just print the value field with the appropriate unit label.

steps.title: Phantom type parameter
steps.explanation: PhantomData<U> tells the compiler Distance uses type U, even though no U value is stored. This prevents mixing Km and Miles.

steps.title: Type-safe construction
steps.explanation: Distance::<Km>::new(100.0) and Distance::<Miles>::new(62.14) are distinct types at compile time.

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: 100.0 prints as "100" and 62.14 prints as "62.14". Each has its unit label.

title: Format Magic
description:
Use the `format!` macro to build a formatted string containing a name, age, and score with 2 decimal places. Then print it.

Expected output:
```
Name: Alice, Age: 25, Score: 98.50
```
hint: Use format!("Name: {}, Age: {}, Score: {:.2}", name, age, score) to create the string. {:.2} formats with 2 decimal places.

steps.title: Use format!
steps.explanation: format! works like println! but returns a String instead of printing. It supports the same formatting syntax.
steps.code: let s = format!("Name: {}, Age: {}, Score: {:.2}", name, age, score);

steps.title: Precision specifier
steps.explanation: {:.2} formats a float with exactly 2 decimal places. 98.5 becomes "98.50".

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: The formatted string is "Name: Alice, Age: 25, Score: 98.50".

title: Write Macro
description:
Use `write!` and a String buffer to build a comma-separated list of numbers. Write the numbers 1 through 5 into a String.

Expected output:
```
1, 2, 3, 4, 5
```
hint: Use write!(buf, "{}", n) for the first element and write!(buf, ", {}", n) for the rest. The Write trait must be in scope.

steps.title: Import Write trait
steps.explanation: std::fmt::Write must be in scope to use write! with a String buffer.

steps.title: Build string with write!
steps.explanation: write! appends formatted text to the buffer. We add ", " before each element except the first.

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: The buffer contains "1, 2, 3, 4, 5" after the loop.

title: Assert Check
description:
Write a function `is_palindrome(s: &str) -> bool` that checks if a string is a palindrome. Use `assert!` and `assert_eq!` to verify test cases, then print the result.

Expected output:
```
"racecar" is a palindrome: true
"hello" is a palindrome: false
```
hint: Compare the string with its reverse: s.chars().rev().collect::<String>() == s.

steps.title: Reverse and compare
steps.explanation: Collect the reversed characters into a String, then compare with the original.
steps.code:
let rev: String = s.chars().rev().collect();
s == rev

steps.title: Assert correctness
steps.explanation: assert_eq! panics if values don't match, assert! panics if false. These verify our function before printing.

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: "racecar" reversed is "racecar" (true), "hello" reversed is "olleh" (false). Assertions pass.

title: Debug Format
hint: Use {:?} for compact debug output and {:#?} for pretty-printed debug output.

steps.title: Derive Debug
steps.explanation: #[derive(Debug)] automatically implements the Debug trait, enabling {:?} formatting.
steps.code:
#[derive(Debug)]
struct Point { x: f64, y: f64 }

steps.title: Compact debug
steps.explanation: {:?} prints in a single line: Point { x: 3.0, y: 4.0 }.

steps.title: Pretty debug
steps.explanation: {:#?} formats with indentation across multiple lines, useful for complex structures.

title: Pad Align
description:
Use format specifiers to demonstrate padding and alignment. Print a word right-aligned in 10 chars, left-aligned in 10 chars, and center-aligned in 10 chars, each surrounded by brackets.

Expected output:
```
[     hello]
[hello     ]
[  hello   ]
```
hint: Use {: >10} for right-align, {: <10} for left-align, and {: ^10} for center-align inside format strings.

steps.title: Right align
steps.explanation: {:>10} pads with spaces on the left to reach width 10, pushing the text right.

steps.title: Left align
steps.explanation: {:<10} pads with spaces on the right. This is also the default for strings.

steps.title: Center align
steps.explanation: {:^10} distributes padding on both sides. With 5 chars in 10 width: 2 left + 3 right spaces.

title: Caesar Cipher
description:
Write a function `caesar_encrypt(text: &str, shift: u8) -> String` that shifts each lowercase letter by `shift` positions (wrapping around). Non-lowercase characters stay unchanged.

Encrypt "hello rust" with shift 3.

Expected output:
```
khoor uxvw
```
hint: For each char, check if it's lowercase. If so, subtract 'a', add shift, mod 26, add 'a' back. Use `as u8` and `as char` for conversion.

steps.title: Map each character
steps.explanation: Iterate over characters and transform each one individually.

steps.title: Shift lowercase letters
steps.explanation: Subtract b'a' to get 0-25, add shift, mod 26 to wrap, add b'a' back to get the ASCII code.
steps.code: let shifted = (c as u8 - b'a' + shift) % 26 + b'a';

steps.title: Preserve other chars
steps.explanation: Spaces and non-lowercase chars pass through unchanged. 'h'+3='k', 'e'+3='h', etc.

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: h->k, e->h, l->o, l->o, o->r, (space stays), r->u, u->x, s->v, t->w. Output: "khoor uxvw".

title: ROT13 Cipher
description:
Write a function `rot13(text: &str) -> String` that applies ROT13 to each ASCII letter (both upper and lowercase). Non-letter characters stay unchanged.

Expected output:
```
Uryyb, Ehfg!
```
hint: ROT13 shifts by 13. Handle both uppercase (A-Z) and lowercase (a-z) separately using b'A'/b'a' as the base.

steps.title: Match on ranges
steps.explanation: Use Rust's range patterns 'a'..='z' and 'A'..='Z' to handle lowercase and uppercase separately.

steps.title: Apply ROT13
steps.explanation: Shift by 13 with wrapping: subtract base, add 13, mod 26, add base back.
steps.code: ((c as u8 - b'a' + 13) % 26 + b'a') as char

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: H->U, e->r, l->y, l->y, o->b gives "Uryyb". Comma and space unchanged. R->E, u->h, s->f, t->g gives "Ehfg!".

title: Bracket Check
description:
Write a function `is_balanced(s: &str) -> bool` that checks if parentheses, brackets, and braces are properly balanced.

Test with `"{[()]}"` (balanced) and `"{[(])}"` (unbalanced).

Expected output:
```
{[()]}: true
{[(])}: false
```
hint: Use a Vec as a stack. Push opening brackets, pop on closing brackets and check they match. Return true if stack is empty at the end.

steps.title: Stack-based approach
steps.explanation: Use a Vec as a stack. Push opening brackets and pop when we see closing ones.

steps.title: Match and validate
steps.explanation: For each closing bracket, pop the stack and verify it matches. If not, return false immediately.

steps.title: Check empty stack
steps.explanation: After processing all chars, the stack must be empty for the string to be balanced.

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: "{[()]}" is properly nested (true). "{[(])}" has mismatched brackets (false).

title: RLE Encoder
description:
Write a function `rle_encode(s: &str) -> String` that performs run-length encoding. Consecutive identical characters are replaced with the character followed by its count.

Encode "aaabbbccddddee".

Expected output:
```
a3b3c2d4e2
```
hint: Track the current character and its count. When the character changes (or at end of string), append the char and count to the result.

steps.title: Track runs
steps.explanation: Compare each character with the previous one. If they match, increment the counter.

steps.title: Emit on change
steps.explanation: When the character changes, append the previous char and its count to the result, then reset the counter.

steps.title: Handle last run
steps.explanation: After the loop, don't forget to emit the last character group.

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: aaa->a3, bbb->b3, cc->c2, dddd->d4, ee->e2. Output: "a3b3c2d4e2".

title: Simple Calc
description:
Write a function `calculate(a: f64, op: char, b: f64) -> Option<f64>` that performs basic arithmetic (+, -, *, /). Return None for division by zero or unknown operators.

Test several operations and print results.

Expected output:
```
10 + 3 = 13
10 - 3 = 7
10 * 3 = 30
10 / 3 = 3.3333
10 / 0 = error
```
hint: Use a match on the operator char. For division, check if b is 0.0 first. Return Some(result) for valid operations.

steps.title: Match on operator
steps.explanation: Use a match expression on the char to handle each arithmetic operation.
steps.code:
match op {
    '+' => Some(a + b),
    '-' => Some(a - b),
    '*' => Some(a * b),
    ...

steps.title: Handle division safely
steps.explanation: Check for division by zero and return None. Otherwise return Some(a / b).
steps.code: '/' => if b == 0.0 { None } else { Some(a / b) },

steps.title: Format results
steps.explanation: For whole numbers, cast to i32 for clean output. For fractional results, use {:.4} for 4 decimal places.
steps.code: if r.fract() == 0.0 { println!("...", r as i32); }
